title: Modern R for Public Health Practitioners
text:
Welcome
This is the website for the Connecticut Department of Public Health’s Data Management, Analytics, and Governance (DMAG) R training for public health practitioners.

title: Modern R for Public Health Practitioners
text:
Special Thanks

Gary Archambault and the rest of the DMAG team
Mukhtar Mohamed
Sydney Jones

title: Modern R for Public Health Practitioners
text:
About the Author
Epidemiologist turned Data Scientist with a decade of R experience.

title: 1  Adopting an R Project Mindset
crumbs: 1  Adopting an R Project Mindset

title: 2  How to use this book
text:
2.1 The big idea
This course is not about teaching you epidemiology. It’s about giving you the R fluency to apply what you already know.
We will dive straight into a data analysis project and with every chapter will we iteratively polish our analysis until at the very end we have something that could be confidently deployed in day to day use.
crumbs: 2  How to use this book

title: 2  How to use this book
text:
2.2 The arc
WIP - PLACEHOLDER








Chapter
What you build
The new skill




0 — Orientation
Your first rendered R analysis
R mindset; the SAS→R Rosetta stone


1 — Import
A real CT dataset + data profile
readr; inspect before you trust


2 — Wrangling
A clean, joined, analysis-ready dataset
tidyverse DATA-step verbs; joins


3 — Descriptive epi
Publication-quality tables
gt; counts, rates, CIs


4 — Visualization
Trend charts + a CT choropleth map
ggplot2; sf + tigris


5 — Quarto report
Leadership-ready report v1
Quarto; findings before methods


6 — Functions
One template, any county or year
Functions; Quarto params


7 — API
A self-fetching, live-data report
SODA API; SoQL; fallback caching


8 — Reproducibility
Published GitHub Pages capstone
renv; here; GitHub Pages


9 — Automation (stretch)
Scheduled re-render
GitHub Actions; handoff
crumbs: 2  How to use this book

title: 2  How to use this book
text:
2.3 How to use this site
Work through modules in order. Each one has:

Overview — what you’ll build, why it matters, and the SAS concepts it remaps
Lesson — a runnable, narrated .qmd file you execute yourself
Exercises — 3–5 scaffolded problems with explicit “done when” checks
Solutions — available after you’ve tried on your own
crumbs: 2  How to use this book

title: 3  Getting Ready for working with files
text:
3.1 The Setup
Covid-19 challenged Public Health infrastructure in so many multi-faceted ways. The sheer amount of data collected and processed over the pandemic meant that what worked in the beginning of the pandemic was no longer feasible towards the end of the pandemic.
Over the course of this book we will explore Connecticut pandemic data and how to deal with the ever increasing amount of data. We will start simple and do things manually before moving on to refine our analytics methodologies.
crumbs: 3  Getting Ready for working with files

title: 3  Getting Ready for working with files
text:
3.2 Data Intro
We are going to use a snippet of real pandemic-era covid-19 data from the connecticut department of public health’s open data portal1.
The full dataset can be found on the portal2 but we will start simply and use the provided snippet in this
TODO: add evergreen link
repository chapters/working-with-real-data/data/covid-2022.csv
crumbs: 3  Getting Ready for working with files

title: 3  Getting Ready for working with files
text:
3.3 Setup
R has many tools for working with data. Over the years the R community has built up a number of their own tools and one of the most popular, the tidyverse will be our entrypoint into working with data in R.
R comes with a base set of tools and eager epidemiologists can opt to use only the base suite of tool and go out into the world if they so choose. However, many smart developers and epidemiologists have come before us and we do not need to recreate the wheel. R has what are called `packages’ - or collection of ready made code that folks can use to get work done.
TODO: – talk about the CRAN etc etc….
We’re going to slowly introduce the different parts of the tidyverse individually….
TODO: flesh

3.3.1 Installing Packages

# pulls the code down from the CRAN for folks to use and installs on 
# their computer
install.packages(
    "dplyr",  # for manipulating data
    "readr", # for reading data files from a users' computers
    "fs" # for working with files on users' file systems
    )
crumbs: 3  Getting Ready for working with files

title: 4  Reading Comma Delimited Files
text:
4.1 Reading our Comma Delimited File (.csv)
We are going to do a few things here:
# Users can have all sorts of packages on their computer
# but will only need a limited subset of them at any one time
# add these packages to your session with `library()` function.

library(dplyr)
library(readr)
library(fs)

# we have a file - how do we read it in?
# we use fs's path() function to create a path
# we user readr's read_csv() and point it to  our path

csv_path <- path("data/covid-2022.csv")

covid_data <- read_csv(csv_path)
We have our data saved into our session, now what? Let’s take a look at it!
# taking a look at the top few rows
head(covid_data)

# A tibble: 6 × 16

#   number_of_positives <dbl>, number_of_negatives <dbl>,
#   number_of_indeterminates <dbl>

title: 4  Reading Comma Delimited Files
text:
‘Invoke’ the packages we will be using so they are availabe to us.
Create and capture the path to our data file and save inside of an object csv_path.
Read the data into our session from the csv and save inside of an object covid_data.
